Content to reinforce learning outside of class. Mark as done when finished.

Aula 1 -- Who Is Rubens Today

TED Talk
How to Make Diseases Disappear -- Rangan Chatterjee

Accessible medical English with clear American accent. Covers lifestyle medicine concepts relevant to endocrinology practice.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note 5 new expressions.

Podcast
The Doctor's Kitchen Podcast -- Episode on Metabolic Health

Conversational medical English with natural pace. Great for listening comprehension and medical vocabulary in context.

Tip: Listen to 15 minutes during your commute. Focus on how doctors introduce themselves.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Documentary
The Weight of the Nation (HBO)

Diabetes and obesity vocabulary in real context. Medical professionals discuss treatment approaches using language relevant to your specialty.

Tip: Watch Part 1 (30 min). Write down 10 medical terms you recognize.

Aula 2 -- Daily Routines & Clinic Life

Series
New Amsterdam -- Season 1, Episode 1

A hospital drama showing daily medical routines, patient appointments, and team discussions. Great for clinic vocabulary and professional interactions.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note how doctors describe their daily routines and schedules.

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) -- Daily Routines

Short episodes with clear pronunciation and natural use of adverbs of frequency. Perfect for commute listening practice.

Tip: Listen twice. First for general understanding, then note every adverb of frequency you hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
A Day in the Life of a Doctor -- Real Medicine

Real doctors showing their daily routines from morning to night. Authentic medical English with natural pace and clinic vocabulary.

Tip: Watch 10 minutes. Write your own daily schedule in English using the same structure.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 3 -- Talking About the Past

Series
The Good Doctor -- Season 1, Episode 1

A medical drama with clear narration of past events and backstory. Great for hearing Past Simple in context with medical vocabulary.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note every time a character says "I did," "I went," or "I worked."

Podcast
TED Talks Daily -- Stories of Discovery

Speakers share personal stories about past experiences, discoveries, and career-changing moments. Natural use of Past Simple.

Tip: Listen to one episode. Write down 5 irregular past verbs you hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Why I Left Medicine -- Doctors Share Their Stories

Doctors narrate past career decisions using clear Past Simple. Authentic medical stories about research, discoveries, and life-changing experiences.

Tip: Watch 10 minutes. Write 10 sentences about YOUR past using the same structures.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 4 -- Life Experiences

Series
House M.D. -- Season 2, Episode 6: "Spin"

Doctors discuss their experiences and share career milestones. Great for hearing Present Perfect in medical contexts: "Have you ever seen a case like this?"

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Count how many times you hear "have you ever" or "I have never."

Podcast
All Ears English -- Episode 1557: "Present Perfect in Real Life"

Clear, practical explanations of ever, never, already, and yet with real conversational examples. Perfect for reinforcing the grammar from this lesson.

Tip: Listen once, then write 5 sentences about YOUR life using Present Perfect.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED Talk: The Beauty of Being a Polyglot -- Luca Lampariello

A polyglot shares his journey of learning multiple languages. Rubens speaks seven languages, so this talk connects directly to his experience. Rich use of Present Perfect throughout.

Tip: Watch the first 10 minutes. Write down every Present Perfect sentence you hear. How does his journey compare to yours?

Aula 5 -- Describing People & Places

Series
Emily in Paris -- Season 1, Episode 1

An American describes Paris and compares it to Chicago. Rich in adjectives and comparatives: "more romantic than," "the most beautiful." Perfect for hearing how people describe cities.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Write down every adjective Emily uses to describe Paris. How would you describe Belém the same way?

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) -- Describing Places

Short, clear episode about how to describe places using adjectives and comparatives. Perfect length for busy professionals. Natural British and American accents.

Tip: Listen once, then describe Belém in 6 sentences using comparatives and superlatives.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Geography Now! -- Brazil

An entertaining, detailed description of Brazil using rich adjectives and comparisons. The host compares regions, climates, and cities. Great for hearing superlatives like "the largest," "the most diverse."

Tip: Watch the section about the North region. How does the host describe the Amazon? Write 5 sentences comparing Belém to another city you know.

Lesson 6 -- Making Plans

Series
The Last Leg / Departures -- any travel-planning episode

People organize a trip on screen: booking flights, choosing a venue, fixing a departure time, and changing plans at the last minute. Listen for every future form: I am going to... for the plan, and I will... for the on-the-spot decisions.

Watch with English subtitles. Write three sentences with going to (plans already decided) and three with will (decisions, offers, predictions). Then describe your own next trip the same way.

Podcast
Zero To Travel -- "How to Plan a Trip" episodes

A relaxed conversation about planning a journey: registration, deadlines, itineraries, and luggage. Full of natural future language for talking about plans and quick decisions.

Note three sentences that describe a future plan. Then record a short plan for your next congress, using at least three new words from today, such as itinerary, deadline, and venue.

YouTube
English with Lucy -- "WILL vs GOING TO"

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: going to for plans and predictions with evidence, will for decisions made at the moment, offers, and promises, and why we never say will to do.

After watching, write five sentences about your upcoming trip: three with going to and two with will. Use registration, booking, and keynote at least once each.

Lesson 7 -- At the Restaurant

Series
Chef's Table / Somebody Feed Phil -- any restaurant episode

Guests order full meals on screen: they choose an appetizer, ask the server for the main course, mention an allergy, and ask for the bill. Listen for polite ordering language: I would like..., could I have...?, and what do you recommend?

Watch with English subtitles. Write three polite requests with would like or could I have, and one question with what do you recommend. Then order your own imaginary dinner the same way.

Podcast
The Splendid Table -- episodes about eating out

A relaxed conversation about food and restaurants: choosing a main course, a side dish, and a dessert, talking about a spicy dish, and leaving a tip. Full of natural language for ordering politely.

Note three sentences that describe ordering or recommending food. Then record a short order for dinner using at least three new words, such as appetizer, side dish, and beverage.

YouTube
English with Lucy -- "How to Order Food in English"

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: would like for polite requests, could I have and can I have for polite questions, asking what do you recommend, and useful restaurant words like bill, tip, and refill.

After watching, write five sentences for a meal: two with would like, two with could I have, and one question with what do you recommend. Use appetizer, dessert, and bill at least once each.

Lesson 8 -- Block 1 Review

Series
The Last Dance -- any episode (sports documentary)

Athletes and coaches reflect on their journey: they describe their progress, their strengths and weaknesses, and the mistakes they have learned from. Listen for the present perfect (we have improved, I have learned) used to talk about a career up to now.

Watch with English subtitles. Write three present perfect sentences from the episode, then describe your own English progress the same way using have or has plus a past participle.

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show -- any guest interview

Guests reflect on how they have improved over the years, the goals they have set, and the feedback that helped them grow. A great model for talking about progress, strengths, and weaknesses in natural English.

Note three sentences where the guest talks about progress or a goal. Then record a short reflection on your own learning, using progress, feedback, and goal at least once each.

YouTube
English with Lucy -- "Present Perfect vs Past Simple"

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the present perfect for progress up to now (I have improved), for and since, and how it is different from the simple past for finished moments (last week I started).

After watching, write five sentences about your English: three with the present perfect and for or since, and two in the simple past with a finished time. Use progress, strength, and goal at least once each.

Aula 9 -- Healthy Habits -- Giving Advice

Tema da aula: Healthy Habits (conselhos com should, shouldn't, must e mustn't). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Documentary
Live to 100: Secrets of the Blue Zones (Netflix)

A doctor travels the world to study communities where people live longest. Listen for the healthy habits they repeat: balanced diet, daily movement, low stress, and strong routines -- exactly today's vocabulary.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada conselho como uma frase com should ou must (ex: "You should eat more vegetables").

YouTube
TED-Ed -- "The benefits of a good night's sleep"

A short, clear animation about why sleep helps your body recover and prevents health problems. Simple English with great vocabulary about wellbeing, stress and habits.

Dica: pause após cada ideia e repita em voz alta. Depois dê três conselhos sobre sono usando should e shouldn't.

Aula 10 -- Giving Directions

Tema da aula: Giving Directions (imperativos e preposições de lugar). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Good Doctor -- any hospital scene (Sony/streaming)

A medical drama set in a large hospital. Watch a scene where staff move between floors, departments and corridors, and listen for directions: go straight, turn left, take the elevator, next to, opposite.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada direção como um imperativo (ex: "Take the elevator to the second floor").

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Giving directions"

A short, clear lesson on asking for and giving directions in English. Simple sentences with turn left, turn right, go straight, next to and opposite -- exactly today's grammar and vocabulary.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e repita em voz alta. Depois dê três direções da entrada até o seu consultório.

Aula 11 -- Shopping & Services

Tema da aula: Shopping & Services (how much/many, substantivos contáveis e incontáveis). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Superstore -- any shopping scene (NBC/streaming)

A comedy set in a large store. Watch a scene with customers and cashiers and listen for shopping language: how much, how many, in stock, discount, receipt, pay in cash.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada vez que alguém pergunta "how much" ou "how many".

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How much and how many"

A short, clear lesson on countable and uncountable nouns and the difference between how much and how many -- exactly today's grammar.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga em voz alta se o substantivo é contável (how many) ou incontável (how much).

Aula 12 -- On the Phone

Tema da aula: On the Phone (expressões de telefone e perguntas indiretas). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Office (US) -- any phone-call scene (NBC/streaming)

An office comedy full of phone calls. Watch a scene where someone answers or transfers a call and listen for phone language: speaking, hold on, connect, leave a message, extension.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada vez que alguém diz "speaking", "hold on" ou "leave a message".

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Indirect questions"

A short, clear lesson on how to make polite indirect questions (Could you tell me...? Do you know if...?) -- exactly today's grammar for the phone.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e transforme uma pergunta direta numa indireta em voz alta.

Aula 13 -- Weather & Small Talk

Tema da aula: Weather & Small Talk (present continuous e vocabulário de clima). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Friends -- any coffee-shop scene at Central Perk (NBC/streaming)

The characters meet for coffee and chat about small, everyday things. Listen for natural small talk and how they break the ice and keep a friendly conversation going.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases curtas que as pessoas usam para puxar conversa.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Talking about the weather"

A short, clear lesson with weather vocabulary and present continuous (it is raining, the sun is shining) -- exactly today's grammar and words.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e descreva o tempo de hoje em Belém em voz alta usando o present continuous.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Making small talk"

A friendly episode about starting conversations with new people: breaking the ice, safe topics like the weather, and keeping a chat going. Perfect for networking at a medical congress.

Dica: ouça duas vezes e anote duas formas de começar uma conversa e duas perguntas leves.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "On the telephone"

A friendly episode that practices phone language: answering, asking to speak to someone, leaving a message and scheduling. As a doctor who calls clinics and patients, you will use this often.

Dica: ouça duas vezes e anote três expressões de telefone e duas perguntas indiretas.

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Shopping and asking for prices"

A friendly episode that practices the language of shopping: asking about quantity, prices, discounts and how to pay. As a doctor who orders supplies, you will use this every month.

Dica: ouça duas vezes e anote três perguntas sobre quantidade (how much / how many) e duas formas de pagar (in cash / by card).

Podcast
Coffee Break English -- "Asking for and giving directions"

A friendly episode that practices the language of directions in everyday situations: streets, buildings and floors. As a doctor, you will recognize the prepositions of place you use with patients every day.

Dica: ouça duas vezes e anote três instruções com imperativos (go, turn, take) e duas preposições de lugar (next to, opposite).

Podcast
Feel Better, Live More -- Dr Rangan Chatterjee

A British doctor talks with experts about simple habits for better health: sleep, food, movement and stress. As a fellow doctor, you will recognize the advice -- and the medical vocabulary in English.

Dica: escolha um episódio curto, ouça duas vezes e anote três conselhos que você daria a um paciente, usando must e mustn't.

The Dream Packing Words The Code Getting There Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- General English

Rubens Tofolo at the
International Medical Conference

Diagnostic Conversation and English Reactivation

B1- -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Tell me three things about yourself

Something professional -- about your work
Something personal -- about your life
Something surprising -- that people do not expect
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your English Challenges

"What is the hardest part of using English at conferences?"
"When was the last time you wanted to say something in English but could not?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

What do you recall about your time in California?

"Tell me about your life in California between 1994 and 1996."
"How was your English back then compared to now?"
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Your Professional
Vocabulary

Eight words and expressions for self-introduction

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Let Us Build Your
Conference Vocabulary

Eight essential words for professional self-introduction

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To focus on one particular area of expertise
Specialize
To focus on one particular area of expertise
"I specialize in endocrinology."
Your education, training, and professional experience
Background
Your education, training, and professional experience
"My background includes research in the US."
At the present time, right now
Currently
At the present time, right now
"I am currently leading a study."
The ability to speak a language smoothly and naturally
Fluency
The ability to speak a language smoothly and naturally
"She achieved fluency after living abroad."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A large formal meeting for professional discussion
Conference
A large formal meeting for professional discussion
"The conference attracted specialists from forty countries."
To remember something from the past
Recall
To remember something from the past
"I can recall presenting in California."
Complete, including all necessary details
Comprehensive
Complete, including all necessary details
"We need a comprehensive review."
To make something active or working again
Reactivate
To make something active or working again
"He wants to reactivate his English."
0 / 4 words revealed
Pronunciation

Say It Right

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice. Pay attention to the stress.

SPE-cia-lize"I specialize in endocrinology."
BACK-ground"My background includes research."
CUR-rent-ly"I am currently leading a study."
CON-fe-rence"The conference starts tomorrow."
com-pre-HEN-sive"A comprehensive review."
Key Expressions

Professional Phrases

"I specialize in endocrinology and obesity management."
"I have been practicing for over twenty-five years."
"I currently focus on diabetes and obesity."
"My background includes research in the United States."
"I want to reactivate my English fluency."
Substitution Drill

Make It Yours

Replace the highlighted parts with your own information:

"I specialize in ___________."

"I have been practicing for ___________ years."

"My background includes ___________."

"I currently focus on ___________."

Vocabulary Check

8 Words Learned

Can you define each one? Let us check.

Specialize Background Currently Fluency Conference Recall Comprehensive Reactivate
Chapter 3: The Code

The Grammar
Code

Present Simple vs Present Perfect

Chapter 3: The Code

What Do These Structures Have in Common?

"I specialize in endocrinology." (fact)
"I currently focus on diabetes." (present routine)
"I have visited ninety-five countries." (life experience)
"I have been practicing for twenty-five years." (duration until now)
TenseUseSignals
Present SimpleFacts, routinesevery day, usually, always
Present PerfectExperience, durationfor, since, ever, never
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I work here since 2001."
"I have worked here since 2001."

Use Present Perfect with for and since when the action continues to the present.

Grammar Practice

Fill In the Blank

"I ___specialize in endocrinology." (fact)
"I ___have visited ninety-five countries." (life experience)
"I ___currently focus on diabetes." (present routine)
"I ___have been practicing for over twenty-five years." (duration)
"The conference ___starts tomorrow morning." (scheduled event)
Substitution Drill

Present Simple or Present Perfect?

"I ___ (work) every day." fact/routine

"I ___ (work) here for twenty years." duration

"I ___ (visit) many countries." experience

"I ___ (specialize) in endocrinology." permanent fact

Chapter 4: Getting There

Meeting Dr. Sarah Chen at the
Conference

A networking conversation in context

Dialogue

At the Conference

SC
"Hi there! I am Dr. Sarah Chen from Johns Hopkins. Are you attending the plenary session on metabolic health?"
RT
"Yes, I am! I specialize in endocrinology. I am very interested in the new research."
SC
"That is great! Where are you based?"
RT
"I have a clinic in Belem, in northern Brazil. I currently focus on diabetes and obesity."
SC
"Interesting! How long have you been in the field?"
RT
"I have been practicing for over twenty-five years. My background includes research in the United States and France."
SC
"Impressive! A doctorate from France. In French or English?"
RT
"In French, actually. I speak seven languages, but I want to reactivate my English fluency for conferences like this."
Comprehension

About Dr. Sarah Chen

Where is Dr. Sarah Chen from?
She is from Johns Hopkins (university/hospital).
What session is Sarah attending?
The plenary session on metabolic health.
What impressed Sarah about Rubens?
His doctorate from France and his extensive experience.
Artifact

Your Conference Badge

International Endocrinology Congress 2026

Dr. Rubens Tofolo

Endocrinologist

Private Clinic -- Belem, Brazil

Specialization: Diabetes & Obesity Management

Languages: Portuguese, English, French, Spanish, Italian, German, Japanese

ATTENDEE -- Full Access

Badge Comprehension

Reading the Badge

What is Dr. Tofolo's specialization?
He specializes in diabetes and obesity management.
How many languages does he speak?
He speaks seven languages.
What type of access does his badge provide?
Full access as an attendee.
Listening

Listen to the Full Introduction

Listen first. Questions will appear after the audio.

Full Self-Introduction
0:000:00
What does Rubens specialize in?
Diabetes and obesity management.
Where did Rubens do research?
In the United States and France.
Chapter 5: Practice

More Practice

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Listening 2

Conference Networking

Listen to the complete dialogue between Rubens and Dr. Chen.

Conference Dialogue
0:000:00
Where is Dr. Chen from?
Johns Hopkins.
How many languages does Rubens speak?
Seven languages.
Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Someone at a conference asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am Dr. Tofolo. I specialize in endocrinology and obesity management. I am based in Belem, Brazil."
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"I work here since 2001."
"I have worked here since 2001."
"I have a clinic in Belem since twenty years."
"I have had a clinic in Belem for twenty years."
"I am specializing in endocrinology." (permanent fact)
"I specialize in endocrinology."
"I visited ninety-five countries in my life."
"I have visited ninety-five countries in my life."
0 / 4 errors found
Oral Drilling

Respond to Each Situation

A researcher asks: "What is your specialty?"
"I specialize in endocrinology and obesity management."
Someone asks: "How long have you been a doctor?"
"I have been practicing for over twenty-five years."
A colleague asks: "Where did you study?"
"My background includes research in the United States and France."
Someone asks: "Why are you at this conference?"
"I want to reactivate my English fluency for international conferences."
A delegate asks: "What are you working on now?"
"I currently focus on diabetes and obesity management at my clinic in Belem."
Someone says: "Tell me something interesting about yourself."
"I speak seven languages and I have visited ninety-five countries."
Sentence Building

Build Your Sentences

Combine these elements into complete sentences using the correct tense:

I / specialize / endocrinology ___"I specialize in endocrinology."
I / visit / 95 countries ___"I have visited ninety-five countries."
I / practice / medicine / 25 years ___"I have been practicing medicine for over twenty-five years."
I / currently / focus / diabetes ___"I currently focus on diabetes."
Grammar Check

Final Grammar Practice

"I ___ (live) in California from 1994 to 1996." ___lived (completed past action)
"I ___ (be) a doctor for 25 years." ___have been (duration to now)
"I ___ (speak) seven languages." ___speak (permanent fact)
"I ___ (want) to improve my English." ___want (current desire)
Pronunciation Focus

Stress Patterns

"I SPE-cia-lize in en-do-cri-NO-lo-gy."

"I have been PRAC-ti-cing for over TWEN-ty-five years."

"We need a com-pre-HEN-sive re-VIEW."

Chapter 6: Your Turn

From Guided to Free

Show what you learned

Role-Play 1: Guided

Conference Registration

Scenario: Conference Registration Desk

You arrive at the International Endocrinology Congress. Go to the registration desk and introduce yourself. The receptionist will ask for your information.

Dr. Tofolo endocrinology Belem, Brazil 25 years diabetes
Role-Play 2: Semi-Free

Coffee Break Networking

Scenario: Coffee Break at the Conference

You meet Dr. Kim from South Korea during a coffee break. Have a natural conversation about your work, experience, and why you are at the conference. Use partial prompts below.

specialize background currently
Role-Play 3: Free

Conference Dinner

Scenario: Dinner Conversation -- No Support

You are at the conference dinner. An American researcher sits next to you and starts a conversation. Talk freely about yourself, your career, your travels, and your goals with English. No keywords, no prompts -- just natural conversation.

Error Correction

Let Us Fix Together

Errors from Today's Practice

Type the errors you noticed during the role-plays:

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

What Did We Learn?

Review and celebrate

What I Learned

Today's Checklist

I can introduce myself professionally at a conference
I know 8 key words for self-introduction
I understand when to use Present Simple vs Present Perfect
I can have a networking conversation at a conference
I can talk about my career and background in English
I can use "currently" to describe what I am doing now
I can ask someone to repeat or speak slower
I can say how long I have been doing something

Badge Earned!

Reactivation -- Lesson 1 Complete

Lesson Complete

Day 1 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Daily Routines & Clinic Life

Lesson 2

Daily Routines & Clinic Life

A day in the life of Dr. Tofolo

Chapter 1: The Alarm

Quick Recall

1. What do you specialize in?

2. How long have you been practicing medicine?

3. What time did you wake up today?

Chapter 1: The Alarm

Your Typical Day

What does your morning routine look like?

How many patients do you see every day?

What do you usually do after work?

Chapter 2

Packing Words

8 essential words for daily routines

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Vocabulary Challenge

Click each card to reveal the word. Can you guess from the clue?

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A regular series of actions you do every day
Routine
A regular series of daily actions
"My morning routine starts at six o'clock."
A scheduled meeting with a patient or colleague
Appointment
A scheduled meeting with a patient
"I have six appointments every morning."
The regular trip between home and work
Commute
The trip between home and work
"My commute takes about thirty minutes."
Identifying a patient's illness or condition
Diagnosis
The identification of a patient's condition
"The diagnosis usually takes one consultation."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A plan of activities organized by time
Schedule
A plan organized by time
"My schedule is always full on Mondays."
To officially recommend a medicine or treatment
Prescribe
To recommend a medicine or treatment
"I usually prescribe metformin for type two diabetes."
A person you work with professionally
Colleague
A person you work with
"I often discuss cases with my colleagues."
A return visit to check a patient's progress
Follow-up
A return visit to check progress
"I always schedule a follow-up after the first consultation."
0 / 4 words revealed

Pronunciation Focus

Routine
rou-TINE
Appointment
a-POINT-ment
Commute
com-MUTE
Diagnosis
di-ag-NO-sis
Schedule
SKED-jool
Prescribe
pre-SCRIBE
Colleague
COL-league
Follow-up
FOL-low-up

Key Expressions

"My morning routine starts at five-thirty."

"I have six appointments every morning."

"I usually prescribe metformin for type two diabetes."

"I always schedule a follow-up after the first consultation."

Make It Personal

Replace the highlighted words with your real information.

"My commute takes about ___ minutes."

"I usually see ___ patients every morning."

"I always / never eat lunch at the clinic."

Quick Review

I say the definition. You say the word.

"A regular series of daily actions"

R_______

"A scheduled meeting with a patient"

A_______

"To recommend a medicine"

P_______

"A return visit to check progress"

F_______

Chapter 3

The Code

Grammar Discovery

Observe the Pattern

"I always wake up at five-thirty."

"He usually sees six patients every morning."

"I often prescribe lifestyle changes."

"She never skips her morning exercise."

What do the green words have in common?

Present Simple + Adverbs of Frequency

We use Present Simple for habits, routines, and facts. Adverbs of frequency go BEFORE the main verb (but AFTER the verb "be").

Third person (he/she/it): add -s to the verb.

Common Mistake

Wrong

"I go always to the clinic at seven."

Correct

"I always go to the clinic at seven."

Rule: Adverb of frequency goes BEFORE the main verb.

But after "be": "I am always busy on Mondays."

Grammar Practice

Place the adverb in the correct position. Click to check.

"I ___ wake up at five-thirty." (always)

"I always wake up at five-thirty."

"He ___ sees patients in the afternoon." (rarely)

"He rarely sees patients in the afternoon."

"My schedule ___ full on Mondays." (is usually)

"My schedule is usually full on Mondays."

"We ___ discuss cases after lunch." (often)

"We often discuss cases after lunch."

"She ___ skip___ her morning exercise." (never / -s)

"She never skips her morning exercise."

How Often?

Choose the right adverb for each situation.

"You wake up at 5:30 every single day, no exceptions."

I ___ wake up at five-thirty. (always)

"You eat at the clinic about 4 days a week."

I ___ eat lunch at the clinic. (usually)

"You finish before 7 PM maybe once a month."

I ___ finish before seven. (rarely)

Chapter 4

Getting There

Context and Dialogue

Conference Coffee Break

Dr. Sarah Chen asks Rubens about his daily routine at the clinic.

SC

Sarah

"So, Rubens, what does a typical day look like at your clinic?"

RT

Rubens

"Well, I always wake up at five-thirty. My commute takes about thirty minutes."

SC

Sarah

"That is early! How many patients do you see?"

RT

Rubens

"I usually see six to eight patients every morning. Each appointment takes about forty minutes."

SC

Sarah

"And what do you do in the afternoons?"

RT

Rubens

"I often meet with colleagues to discuss cases. I also schedule follow-ups and review lab results."

SC

Sarah

"Do you ever finish early?"

RT

Rubens

"I rarely finish before seven. But I never skip my morning exercise. That is my rule!"

Dialogue Check

1. How long does Rubens's commute take?

About thirty minutes.

2. How many patients does he usually see every morning?

Six to eight patients.

3. What does he never skip?

His morning exercise.

Dr. Tofolo's Daily Schedule

Daily Schedule

Dr. Rubens Tofolo

Endocrinology Clinic, Belém

05:30Wake up + Morning exercise
06:30Coffee + Medical journals
07:00Commute to clinic
07:30Morning appointments (6-8 patients)
12:30Lunch
14:00Colleagues meeting + Case discussion
15:30Follow-ups + Lab results
19:00End of day

Schedule Questions

1. What does Rubens do at six-thirty?

He has coffee and reads medical journals.

2. When does he meet with colleagues?

At two o'clock in the afternoon.

3. What time does his day end?

At seven o'clock in the evening.

Listening Challenge

Listen to Dr. Tofolo describing his daily routine. Answer the questions after.

0:00 / 0:00

1. What time does the routine start?

Five-thirty in the morning.

2. What does he prescribe for diabetes?

Metformin and lifestyle changes.

Chapter 5

Practice

Put it all together

Listening 2: The Full Dialogue

Listen to the complete conversation between Rubens and Sarah.

0:00 / 0:00

1. What does Sarah ask first?

What a typical day looks like at his clinic.

2. How often does Rubens finish before seven?

Rarely.

Quick Fire

0 / 6

Question 1 of 6

A colleague asks: "What does your morning look like?" Describe your morning routine.

"I always wake up at five-thirty. I usually have coffee and read medical journals. My commute takes about thirty minutes."

Spot the Error

0 / 4 errors found

"He go always to the clinic at seven."

"He always goes to the clinic at seven."

"I am seeing usually six patients every morning."

"I usually see six patients every morning."

"She never don't skip exercise."

"She never skips exercise."

"My commute take about thirty minutes."

"My commute takes about thirty minutes."

Oral Practice

Describe what you do at seven-thirty every morning.

"I usually see my first patient at seven-thirty. Each appointment takes about forty minutes."

Tell me about your afternoons at the clinic.

"I often meet with colleagues to discuss cases. I also schedule follow-ups and review lab results."

What do you never skip, even on busy days?

"I never skip my morning exercise. I always run before work."

How often do you finish before seven PM?

"I rarely finish before seven in the evening."

Build the Sentence

Combine these elements into correct sentences.

I / usually / prescribe / metformin

"I usually prescribe metformin."

He / never / skip / exercise

"He never skips exercise."

My schedule / be / always / full

"My schedule is always full."

We / often / discuss / cases / after lunch

"We often discuss cases after lunch."

Complete the Sentences

"I ___ (always / wake up) at five-thirty."

"I always wake up at five-thirty."

"She ___ (often / meet) with colleagues."

"She often meets with colleagues."

"The diagnosis ___ (usually / take) one consultation."

"The diagnosis usually takes one consultation."

"Do you ___ (ever / finish) before seven?"

"Do you ever finish before seven?"

Pronunciation Focus

"My schedule is always full on Mondays."

Focus: SKED-jool (American pronunciation)

"My commute takes about thirty minutes."

Focus: take-S (voiceless S), THIR-ty (TH sound)

"He never skips his morning exercise."

Focus: skip-S (consonant cluster)

"I usually prescribe metformin."

Focus: pre-SCRIBE (stress on 2nd syllable)

Chapter 6

Your Turn

Free Production

Role-Play: Guided

Visiting Doctor

A colleague from Canada visits your clinic

"A Canadian endocrinologist is visiting your clinic for a week. They ask you to describe your daily routine so they can shadow you."

routine appointment usually always schedule

Role-Play: Semi-Free

New Resident

A medical resident on their first day

"A new medical resident starts today. Explain your complete daily routine, including what you do, when, and how often. Help them understand how the clinic works."

commute follow-up prescribe

Role-Play: Free

Health Journalist

An interview about a doctor's daily life

"A journalist from a health magazine is writing an article called 'A Day in the Life of a Doctor in Northern Brazil.' They want to know everything about your daily routine, from morning to evening. Speak freely."

Error Correction

Errors from the role-plays. Let us fix them together.

Click here and type the errors you noticed during the role-plays...

Chapter 7

Wrap Up

What I Learned

I can describe my daily routine using Present Simple
I can use adverbs of frequency in the correct position
I know 8 new words: routine, appointment, commute, diagnosis, schedule, prescribe, colleague, follow-up
I can talk about my clinic routine with a colleague
I can add -s correctly for third person singular

Badge Earned!

Daily Routines -- Lesson 2 Complete

Next Lesson Preview

Lesson 3

Talking About the Past

Past Simple (regular and irregular verbs)

Share your California memories

Lesson Complete

Day 2 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Talking About the Past

Lesson 3

California Memories

Talking about the past with Dr. Tofolo

Warm-up

From your present routines to your past experiences.

Callback: "Describe your morning routine in two sentences."

Transition: "Now think about the past. You lived in California. What was different?"

Your Past

Answer freely. Do not worry about grammar yet.

"When did you first leave Brazil?"

"What was the best trip you ever took?"

"Where did you study for your doctorate?"

Chapter 2

Packing Words

8 essential words for past experiences

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Vocabulary Challenge

Click each card to reveal the word. Can you guess from the clue?

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A systematic investigation to discover new facts
Research
A systematic investigation to discover facts
"I conducted research at a university hospital in California."
In or to a foreign country
Abroad
In or to a foreign country
"He lived abroad for two years during his research program."
To find something new or unknown for the first time
Discover
To find something new for the first time
"I discovered new approaches to metabolic syndrome."
To make research or writing available to the public
Publish
To make research available to the public
"She published three papers on diabetes treatment."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

Knowledge or skill gained by doing something
Experience
Knowledge gained by doing something
"The experience changed my entire career."
To go to and be present at an event or meeting
Attend
To be present at an event
"I attended several international conferences."
A favorable situation or chance to do something
Opportunity
A favorable chance to do something
"The opportunity to study abroad was incredible."
To successfully reach a goal through effort
Achieve
To reach a goal through effort
"He achieved his doctorate in France."
0 / 4 words revealed

Pronunciation Focus

Research
re-SEARCH
Abroad
a-BROAD
Discover
dis-CO-ver
Publish
PU-blish
Experience
ex-PE-ri-ence
Attend
a-TEND
Opportunity
op-por-TU-ni-ty
Achieve
a-CHIEVE

Key Expressions

"I conducted research at a university hospital in California."

"I attended several international conferences."

"The opportunity to study abroad was incredible."

"He achieved his doctorate in France."

Make It Personal

Replace the highlighted words with your real information.

"I traveled to ___ in ___."

"I published / attended / discovered ___."

"The experience / opportunity changed ___."

Quick Review

I say the definition. You say the word.

"A systematic investigation"

R_______

"In a foreign country"

A_______

"To make research public"

P_______

"A favorable chance"

O_______

Chapter 3

The Code

Grammar Discovery

Observe the Pattern

"I traveled to California in nineteen ninety-five."

"I worked at a university hospital."

"I met researchers from all over the world."

"I went to several conferences."

What do the green words have in common?

Past Simple

We use Past Simple for completed actions in the past, with a specific time reference.

Regular verbs: add -ed (travel → traveled, work → worked, publish → published)

Irregular verbs: unique forms (go → went, meet → met, have → had, get → got)

Negative: did not (didn't) + base verb. Question: Did + subject + base verb?

Common Mistake

Wrong

"I didn't went to the conference."

Correct

"I didn't go to the conference."

Rule: In negative Past Simple, use didn't + base verb.

Never: didn't + past form.

Grammar Practice

Put the verb in the Past Simple. Click to check.

"I ___ (travel) to California in 1995."

"I traveled to California in 1995."

"She ___ (publish) three papers."

"She published three papers."

"I ___ (meet) researchers from many countries."

"I met researchers from many countries."

"He ___ (go) to France for his doctorate."

"He went to France for his doctorate."

"The experience ___ (change) my career."

"The experience changed my career."

Regular or Irregular?

Give the Past Simple form of each verb.

travel

regular: traveled

go

irregular: went

meet

irregular: met

publish

regular: published

bring

irregular: brought

discover

regular: discovered

take

irregular: took

attend

regular: attended

Chapter 4

Getting There

Context and Dialogue

California Memories

Sarah asks Rubens about his time in California.

SC

Sarah

"You mentioned you lived in California. What was that like?"

RT

Rubens

"It was an incredible experience. I traveled there in nineteen ninety-five for a research program."

SC

Sarah

"How long did you stay?"

RT

Rubens

"I lived there for two years. I worked at a university hospital and published three papers."

SC

Sarah

"That sounds amazing! Did you attend any conferences?"

RT

Rubens

"Yes, I attended several. I met researchers from all over the world."

SC

Sarah

"What did you discover during that time?"

RT

Rubens

"I discovered new approaches to metabolic syndrome. The opportunity changed my entire career."

Dialogue Check

1. When did Rubens travel to California?

In nineteen ninety-five.

2. How many papers did he publish?

Three papers.

3. What did he discover during his research?

New approaches to metabolic syndrome.

Conference Badge, 1995

International Conference on Metabolic Disorders

San Francisco, California -- November 1995

Dr. Rubens Tofolo

Visiting Researcher

Affiliation

University Hospital, California

Specialty

Endocrinology

SPEAKER -- Metabolic Syndrome Panel

Badge Questions

1. Where was the conference held?

In San Francisco, California.

2. What was Rubens's role at the conference?

He was a speaker on the Metabolic Syndrome Panel.

3. What was his affiliation?

University Hospital, California.

Listening Challenge

Listen to Rubens telling his California story. Answer the questions after.

0:00 / 0:00

1. What year did Rubens go to California?

Nineteen ninety-five.

2. What did the experience change?

His entire career.

Chapter 5

Practice

Put it all together

Listening 2: The Full Dialogue

Listen to the complete conversation about California.

0:00 / 0:00

1. Did Rubens attend conferences in California?

Yes, he attended several international conferences.

2. Who did he meet at the conferences?

Researchers from all over the world.

Quick Fire

0 / 6

Question 1 of 6

Someone asks: "Tell me about your time in California." Share your story.

"I traveled to California in nineteen ninety-five. I lived there for two years and conducted research at a university hospital."

Spot the Error

0 / 4 errors found

"I didn't went to the conference."

"I didn't go to the conference."

"I meeted researchers from Japan."

"I met researchers from Japan."

"He taked the opportunity to study abroad."

"He took the opportunity to study abroad."

"I published in California since 1995."

"I published in California in 1995." (Past Simple with a specific time, not "since")

Oral Practice

Tell me about the year you traveled to California.

"I traveled to California in nineteen ninety-five. I went there for a research program."

What did you publish during your time there?

"I published three papers on metabolic syndrome. The research focused on new treatment approaches."

Who did you meet at the conferences?

"I met researchers from all over the world. I made important connections."

How did the California experience change your career?

"I discovered new approaches to metabolic syndrome. The opportunity changed my entire career."

Build the Sentence

Combine these elements into correct Past Simple sentences.

I / travel / California / 1995

"I traveled to California in 1995."

He / meet / researchers / world

"He met researchers from all over the world."

She / publish / three papers / diabetes

"She published three papers on diabetes."

The opportunity / change / career

"The opportunity changed his career."

Complete the Sentences

"I ___ (go) to California in 1995."

"I went to California in 1995."

"She ___ (attend) several conferences."

"She attended several conferences."

"He ___ (bring) new knowledge back to Brazil."

"He brought new knowledge back to Brazil."

"___ you ___ (discover) anything new?"

"Did you discover anything new?"

Pronunciation Focus

"I traveled to California in nineteen ninety-five."

Focus: travel-D (/d/ sound)

"I worked at a university hospital."

Focus: work-T (/t/ sound)

"I attended several international conferences."

Focus: attend-ED (/ɪd/ sound)

"I published three papers during that period."

Focus: publish-T (/t/ sound)

Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free production

Role-Play: Guided

Conference Networking

You meet a fellow researcher at a conference

"A researcher from Germany asks about your career. Share what you did in California, what you published, and who you met. Use Past Simple."

traveled published attended discovered research

Role-Play: Semi-Free

Journalist Interview

A journalist asks about your international career

"A health journalist is writing an article called 'Brazilian Doctors Who Studied Abroad.' Tell them about California, France, and how those experiences shaped your career."

abroad opportunity achieve

Role-Play: Free

Dinner Party Storytelling

Free conversation about travel and experiences

"You are at a dinner party with international colleagues. Someone asks: 'What is the most interesting place you ever lived?' Tell them about your past experiences. Speak freely about California, France, and the ninety-five countries you visited."

Error Correction

Errors from the role-plays. Let us fix them together.

Click here and type the errors you noticed during the role-plays...

Chapter 7

Wrap Up

What I Learned

I can talk about past experiences using Past Simple
I know the difference between regular (-ed) and irregular verbs
I know 8 new words: research, abroad, discover, publish, experience, attend, opportunity, achieve
I can tell someone about my California experience
I can use didn't + base verb for negatives

Badge Earned!

California Memories -- Lesson 3 Complete

Next Lesson Preview

Lesson 4

Life Experiences

Present Perfect for experiences

What have you done in ninety-five countries?

Lesson Complete

Day 3 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Life Experiences

Lesson 4

The World Traveler

Life experiences with Dr. Tofolo

From the Past to Your Whole Life

Last class (Past Simple):

"I traveled to California in 1995."

↓

Today (Present Perfect):

"I have visited ninety-five countries."

What is the most remarkable thing you have ever done?

Think about your 95 countries, 7 languages, or your career milestones.

Chapter 2

Packing Words

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A long trip or the process of personal growth
Journey
A long trip or the process of personal growth
"My journey to becoming a doctor started in medical school."
To travel or search for new places or ideas
Explore
To travel or search for new places or ideas
"I want to explore new treatment methods for diabetes."
An important event or achievement in life
Milestone
An important event or achievement in life
"Getting my doctorate was a major milestone in my career."
Something difficult that tests your ability
Challenge
Something difficult that tests your ability
"Learning seven languages was a real challenge."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To succeed in doing something important
Accomplish
To succeed in doing something important
"I have accomplished many things in twenty-five years of medicine."
To make wider, to expand knowledge or perspective
Broaden
To make wider, to expand knowledge or perspective
"Traveling has broadened my perspective on medicine."
To succeed in dealing with a problem or difficulty
Overcome
To succeed in dealing with a problem or difficulty
"I have overcome many obstacles in my career."
Worthy of attention; unusual or impressive
Remarkable
Worthy of attention; unusual or impressive
"His career has been truly remarkable."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Check

Complete with the right word

"Getting my doctorate was a major _____ in my career."

milestone

"I have _____ many obstacles in my career."

overcome

"Traveling has _____ my perspective on medicine."

broadened

"His career has been truly _____."

remarkable

Chapter 3

The Code

Observe the Pattern

"I have visited ninety-five countries."

"Have you ever attended a conference in Asia?"

"I have already published my latest paper."

"I have never missed a conference deadline."

What do the green words have in common?

Present Perfect

We use Present Perfect for life experiences and actions connected to the present, without a specific time.

Structure: have/has + past participle (visited, attended, published)

ever = at any time (questions). never = at no time (negatives)

already = before now (done). yet = until now (not done, questions/negatives)

Past Simple vs Present Perfect

Past Simple

"I visited France in 1990."

Specific time in the past

Present Perfect

"I have visited France."

Life experience, no specific time

"I published three papers in 1998."

"I have published many papers."

Common Mistake

Wrong

"I have visited France in 1990."

Correct

"I have visited France." or "I visited France in 1990."

With a specific time (in 1990, yesterday, last week), always use Past Simple.

Grammar Practice

"_____ you ever _____ to Japan?" (be)

Have you ever been to Japan?

"I _____ already _____ my presentation." (finish)

I have already finished my presentation.

"She _____ never _____ outside of Europe." (travel)

She has never traveled outside of Europe.

"I _____ visited Africa _____." (not...yet)

I haven't visited Africa yet.

Chapter 4

Getting There

Life Experiences

Sarah and Rubens share their life experiences at the conference.

SC

Sarah

"Rubens, have you ever been to Asia?"

RT

Rubens

"Yes, I have! I have visited Japan, China, and South Korea. Medical conferences there are remarkable."

SC

Sarah

"That is impressive! Have you already explored the conference schedule for tomorrow?"

RT

Rubens

"Not yet. I have been so busy with the networking sessions."

SC

Sarah

"I see! What is the biggest milestone in your career so far?"

RT

Rubens

"Getting my doctorate in France was a major milestone. I have accomplished a lot since then."

SC

Sarah

"Have you ever had to overcome a major challenge?"

RT

Rubens

"Yes, learning to communicate in seven languages was a real challenge. But it has broadened my perspective enormously."

Did You Understand?

1. Which Asian countries has Rubens visited?

Japan, China, and South Korea.

2. What does Sarah ask about the conference schedule?

She asks if Rubens has already explored it for tomorrow.

3. What was the biggest challenge Rubens has overcome?

Learning to communicate in seven languages.

Listening Challenge

Listen to someone sharing their life experiences. Answer the questions after.

0:00 / 0:00

1. How many countries has the speaker visited?

Ninety-five countries.

2. What has broadened his perspective?

Traveling and learning different languages.

Listening Challenge 2

Listen to this conversation about life experiences.

0:00 / 0:00

1. Has the speaker already finished her research?

Not yet, but she has already collected the data.

2. What milestone does she mention?

Publishing her first paper in a major journal.

Conference Badge

RT

Dr. Rubens Tofolo

Endocrinologist | Belém, Brazil

95

Countries Visited

7

Languages Spoken

25+

Years in Medicine

International Medical Conference 2025

Chapter 5

More Practice

Quick Fire

0 / 6

Question 1 of 6

A colleague asks: "Have you ever been to Asia?" Share your experience.

"Yes, I have! I have visited Japan, China, and South Korea. Medical conferences there are remarkable."

Spot the Error

0 / 4 errors found

"I have visited France in 1990."

"I visited France in 1990." or "I have visited France." (no specific time with Present Perfect)

"I have ever been to Japan."

"I have been to Japan." (ever is for questions: "Have you ever been?")

"She has already went home."

"She has already gone home." (have/has + past participle, not past simple)

"I haven't finished already."

"I haven't finished yet." (yet for negatives, already for affirmatives)

Say It Right

"I have visited ninety-five countries."

"Have you ever presented at an international conference?"

"I have already finished my latest research paper."

"I have never missed a conference deadline."

Have You Ever...?

"Have you ever lived in another country?"

Yes, I have. I have lived in France and the United States.

"Have you ever presented at a conference in English?"

Not yet, but I have attended many conferences. I want to present next year.

"Have you ever treated a patient from another country?"

Yes, I have. I have treated patients from several countries in South America.

Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free

Role-Play: Guided

Conference Networking

A doctor asks about your life experiences

"A researcher from Germany asks about your international experiences. Answer using Present Perfect: what countries you have visited, what you have accomplished, and what challenges you have overcome."

95 countries 7 languages doctorate in France metabolic syndrome

Role-Play: Semi-Free

Panel Discussion

You are on a panel sharing career milestones

"A moderator asks: 'What is the most remarkable thing you have accomplished in your career?' Share your journey, including milestones, challenges you have overcome, and how your experiences have broadened your perspective."

have accomplished have overcome has broadened

Role-Play: Free

Free Conversation

Share your experiences freely

"Tell me about your most remarkable life experiences. What have you accomplished? Where have you been? What challenges have you overcome? How has your journey shaped who you are today?"

Error Correction

Errors from the conversation:

Chapter 7

Wrap-Up

Survival Card

1

"I have visited ninety-five countries."

2

"Have you ever attended a medical conference abroad?"

3

"I have already published several papers on metabolic syndrome."

4

"I have never missed a conference deadline."

5

"Traveling has broadened my perspective on medicine."

What I Learned

Today's Checklist

I can talk about life experiences using Present Perfect
I know when to use ever, never, already, and yet
I know 8 new words: journey, explore, milestone, challenge, accomplish, broaden, overcome, remarkable
I understand the difference between Past Simple and Present Perfect
I can ask and answer "Have you ever...?" questions

Badge Earned!

Life Experiences -- Lesson 4 Complete

Lesson Complete

Day 4 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Comparing Past and Present

Day 5 -- Describing People & Places

From Belém to Belém

Describe your city, your people, your world

Warm-up

A Tale of Two Beléms

Last class you talked about your experiences in Lisbon. Let's remember:

"I have already visited the Belem Tower."

"It has been an unforgettable journey."

Now think:

Sofia laughs: "The Belem Tower... and you are from Belém, Brazil! Is your city like Lisbon? Bigger? Hotter?" How do you compare two cities?

Today's Goal

Paint the Picture

1. Describe

lively, humid, charming...

2. Compare

bigger than, more lively than

3. Celebrate

the best açaí in the world

Chapter 2

Describing Words

8 adjectives for people and places

Describing Words

Words 1-4

0 / 4 words revealed

Full of people, with little space

Crowded

"The market is crowded on Saturdays."

Hot air, full of water -- like the Amazon

Humid

"Belém is hot and humid all year."

Full of life, energy, and movement

Lively

"The Ver-o-Peso market is very lively."

Beautiful in a delicate, attractive way

Charming

"The old town is small but charming."

Describing Words

Words 5-8

0 / 4 words revealed

Old and important in history

Historic

"Belém has a historic city center."

Not expensive -- good for your money

Affordable

"Life in Belém is more affordable than in Lisbon."

Friendly to visitors, makes you feel at home

Welcoming

"People in the North are very welcoming."

So beautiful it stops your breath

Breathtaking

"The sunset over the river is breathtaking."

Pronunciation Drill

Say It Clearly

Crowded
Humid
Affordable
Breathtaking
Chapter 3

Comparing Worlds

bigger than · more lively than · the best

Discover the Rule

Listen and Notice

"Belém is hotter than Lisbon."

"Lisbon is more compact than Belém."

"The Amazon is the largest forest in the world."

"Belém has the best açaí in Brazil."

AdjectiveComparativeSuperlative
short: hot, big, largehotter / bigger thanthe hottest / biggest
long: lively, crowded, affordablemore lively thanthe most lively
irregular: good, badbetter / worse thanthe best / the worst

Compare two things with "than". For number one in a group, use "the ...est / the most".

Common Mistake

Right vs Wrong

✗ WRONG

"Belém is more better."

✓ RIGHT

"Belém is better."

✗ WRONG

"It is the most big market."

✓ RIGHT

"It is the biggest market."

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentence

"Belém is _____ than Lisbon." (hot) [show]

"The market is _____ lively than the mall." [show]

"The Amazon is the _____ forest in the world." (large) [show]

"Our açaí is _____ than the açaí in São Paulo." (good) [show]

"The sunset over the river is _____." (stops your breath) [show]

Chapter 4

The Postcard

A piece of Belém for Dr. Sofia

Artifact

Greetings from Belém

POSTCARD · BELÉM, PA

To: Dr. Sofia, Porto

Dear Sofia, greetings from Belém! My city is hotter and more humid than Lisbon, but the people are the most welcoming in Brazil. The Ver-o-Peso is the biggest open-air market in Latin America — crowded, lively, and full of fruits you have never seen. Come visit: the sunset over the river is breathtaking. — Rubens

Listening 1

Sofia's Porto

Listen to Sofia describe her hometown. Close your eyes and focus.

Duration: ~40 seconds

1. Is Porto bigger or smaller than Lisbon?

Click to reveal

2. What is the best thing in Porto for Sofia?

Click to reveal

Listening 2

The Model Description

A tour guide presents Belém to visitors.

1. What is the Ver-o-Peso?

Click to reveal

2. How is the weather in Belém?

Click to reveal

3. What does the guide say about the people?

Click to reveal

Coffee Break

Tell Me About Belém

S
"So you are from Belém! Is it like Lisbon?"
R
"Not really. Belém is hotter and more humid than Lisbon."
S
"Hotter than this summer here? Impossible!"
R
"Believe me! It rains almost every afternoon. But the city is very lively."
S
"What is the most famous place there?"
R
"The Ver-o-Peso market. It is the biggest open-air market in Latin America."
S
"Bigger than the markets here? What can I find there?"
R
"Fish, herbs, fruits you have never seen. It is crowded but charming."
S
"And the people? Are they friendly like here?"
R
"Even friendlier! People from the North are the most welcoming in Brazil."
S
"You are a good salesman for your city! Now I want to visit."
R
"Come! The sunset over the river is breathtaking — the best in the world."
Comprehension

About Dr. Sofia

Does Sofia believe Belém is hotter than Lisbon at first?

Click to reveal

What does Sofia ask about?

Click to reveal

Does Sofia want to visit Belém now?

Click to reveal

Chapter 5

Quick Fire

6 situations -- Answer fast!

Quick Fire

Situation Challenge

1. Compare the weather: Belém vs Lisbon (hot).

Show Answer

2. Say the Amazon is number one in size among forests.

Show Answer

3. Describe the Ver-o-Peso with two adjectives.

Show Answer

4. Compare prices: Belém vs Lisbon (affordable).

Show Answer

5. Say your açaí is number one in Brazil.

Show Answer

6. Describe the people of the North (welcoming, superlative).

Show Answer

Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Belém is more better than Lisbon."

Click to correct

"It is the most big market."

Click to correct

"Belém is hotter that Lisbon."

Click to correct

"The people are most welcoming of Brazil."

Click to correct

Oral Drilling

Repeat After the Audio

"Belém is hotter than Lisbon."

"The market is more lively than the mall."

"It is the biggest market in Latin America."

"The sunset over the river is breathtaking."

Error Correction

Let's Fix These

Errors from today's class:

Chapter 6

Your Turn

Guided > Semi-free > Free

Role-Play: Guided

Sofia's Questions

Scenario: Sofia asks about Belém: the weather, the food, the people. Answer with the adjectives from today.

Keyword chips:

hot and humid lively welcoming
Role-Play: Semi-free

Belém vs Lisbon

Scenario: Compare the two cities for Sofia: weather, size, prices, and food. Which is better for a vacation — and why?

Keywords:

hotter than more affordable than the best
Role-Play: Free

The Voice of Belém

Scenario: A foreign colleague has never been to Brazil. Describe Belém to her: the city, the weather, the market, the food, the people, and the sunset. Make her want to visit!

No keywords. You are the voice of your city!

What I Learned

Lesson 5 Checklist

I can describe a place with adjectives: lively, crowded, charming, breathtaking.
I can compare two cities: "hotter than", "more affordable than".
I can use superlatives: "the biggest", "the most welcoming", "the best".
I described Belém to a foreigner, from the market to the sunset.
I know the words: crowded, humid, lively, charming, historic, affordable, welcoming, breathtaking.

Ambassador Badge Earned!

You are the voice of Belém now, Dr. Rubens.

Day 5 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Making Plans — Going to vs Will

T
